name: Cascade
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site in central Idaho offering agate, jasper, and quartz collecting with easy flat terrain and no permit required.
description:
Cascade sits on Idaho BLM land in Valley County, a region rich with silica-rich mineral deposits from ancient volcanic and hydrothermal activity. This location offers accessible rockhounding opportunities for collectors seeking agates, jasper, and quartz specimens on public land with straightforward regulations and no permitting hassles.

The terrain at Cascade is relatively flat at 4,731 feet elevation, making it suitable for rockhounders of varying experience levels. The area is characterized by typical high-desert sagebrush country interspersed with rocky outcrops and washes where specimens weather out naturally. The flat topography means less strenuous hiking compared to many Idaho rockhounding sites, though you should bring sturdy footwear for walking over uneven rocky ground.

Personal use collection is allowed under BLM regulations with a daily limit of 25 pounds per person. This is ideal for day trips where you can fill a backpack with quality specimens without commercial pressure. The Valley County area experiences four distinct seasons, making spring and fall the most comfortable collecting times when temperatures are moderate and water sources are more predictable.

Locals report finding good-quality jasper and agate in the washes and along hillside exposures. The silica-rich geology of this region, shaped by Miocene-era volcanic activity and subsequent erosion, concentrates these minerals in accessible locations. Plan to spend several hours exploring the area to locate productive collecting spots.
history: This area sits within Idaho's Basin and Range province where Miocene-era volcanic activity and associated hydrothermal systems deposited silica-rich minerals including agate, jasper, and chalcedony throughout the region. Decades of erosion and weathering continue to expose these specimens in surface washes and hillside exposures today.
tips: Search in and around dry washes where water erosion naturally concentrates lighter material and exposes colorful agate and jasper pieces. Look for specimens with translucent banding or distinctive color - Idaho agates often display red, white, and gray patterns. Bring a small rock hammer or chisel to test hardness and break open promising rocks, and use a 5-gallon bucket to haul your daily limit of 25 pounds. Early morning collecting is best as the low sun angle highlights color and banding in specimens.
terrain_notes: Flat high-desert terrain at 4,731 feet elevation covered in sagebrush and scattered rocky outcrops. Walking surface consists of loose rock, gravel, and uneven ground typical of Basin and Range country. Most productive areas are within 1-2 miles of parking, requiring moderate walking over footing that demands attention but poses no major obstacles.
best_season: April - June, September - October. Spring and fall offer mild temperatures ideal for day trips in central Idaho's high-desert climate. Summer brings intense heat and afternoon thunderstorms common to the region. Winter snowfall makes access unreliable.
written_directions: From Cascade town center, head south on ID-55 for approximately 8 miles. Turn east onto BLM road access road and follow it for 2-3 miles to the rockhounding area. Look for pullouts and parking areas near the base of low hills with exposed rocky outcrops. GPS coordinates (44.47138, -115.992795) mark the general collecting area.
road_conditions: BLM roads are typically unpaved and may be washboard in places. Standard passenger vehicles can access main parking areas, but high-clearance is helpful for rougher secondary roads.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along BLM roads near the collecting site. No developed parking lot; space for 3-5 vehicles at typical spots.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection or sales. No motorized excavation or large-scale digging. Collectors must pack out all trash and leave the site in good condition.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
Maximum 25 pounds per person per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No large digging or trenching
Pack out all trash
Leave the area undisturbed for other collectors
nearest_services: Cascade town (approximately 8-10 miles) offers gas, food, and supplies. More extensive services available in McCall (approximately 25 miles north).
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved BLM roads lead to parking areas. The collecting area itself involves walking over uneven rocky ground with loose rock and gravel. Sturdy footwear is essential. No wheelchair access on the natural terrain, but parking areas are drivable for standard vehicles.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find rockhounding near Cascade Idaho?
faq.answer: Cascade area BLM land produces high-quality agates with red, white, and gray banding patterns typical of the Basin and Range province, along with colorful jasper specimens and translucent chalcedony. Most specimens are hand-sized, making them ideal for collectors.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Cascade on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at Cascade. Idaho BLM land allows recreational collection under FLPMA with a 25-pound daily limit per person and no commercial use.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect rocks near Cascade Valley County?
faq.answer: April through June and September through October offer ideal conditions. Spring and fall temperatures are mild, and weather is more predictable than summer (which brings afternoon thunderstorms and heat) or winter (which brings snow and access issues).

faq.question: Can families and children rockhound at Cascade Idaho?
faq.answer: Yes, Cascade is family-friendly for children ages 6 and up. The flat terrain requires minimal hiking, and the casual 25-pound daily limit makes it perfect for family day trips without heavy excavation.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Cascade?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, a small rock hammer or chisel for testing specimens, a 5-gallon bucket for collecting, water, sunscreen, and a hat. The high desert sun is intense even in spring and fall. A magnifying glass helps identify color and banding details.

faq.question: How far is Cascade rockhounding site from the nearest town with gas and food?
faq.answer: Cascade town is approximately 8-10 miles away and offers basic supplies and food. McCall, a larger town with full services, is about 25 miles north. Plan accordingly for water and supplies before heading to the remote BLM collecting areas.
